class_schedule.topic: The Indie Landscape and Success Stories
learning_objectives: Analyze the current indie game market including revenue distribution, genre trends, and platform dynamics
Identify the common traits shared by commercially successful indie games
Distinguish between survivorship bias narratives and realistic market expectations
Evaluate personal readiness for indie development based on skills, resources, and risk tolerance
readings: Blood, Sweat, and Pixels by Jason Schreier, Ch. 1-4
State of Indie Gaming Report 2024 - Game Discoverability Now
Chris Zukowski, 'How That Game Sold on Steam' analysis series

lecture_notes.introduction: Indie game development is simultaneously the most accessible and the most unforgiving path in the games industry. The tools are free, the platforms are open, and a single person can ship a game to millions of potential players from their bedroom. But the market is brutal: over 14,000 games launched on Steam in 2024, and the median lifetime revenue was under $5,000. For every Hollow Knight or Stardew Valley that earns millions, thousands of games of comparable quality sell fewer than 100 copies. This course teaches you to navigate this landscape with eyes open. We will cover every aspect of building and shipping an indie game: scope management, art and audio on a budget, marketing with no budget, crowdfunding, Early Access, community feedback, publishing, business operations, and sustainable development practices. The goal is not to guarantee success, because that is impossible in a market this competitive, but to give you every possible advantage through preparation, strategy, and craft. We begin with an honest assessment of the market because informed optimism is the only sustainable foundation for indie development.
key_concepts: Market reality by the numbers: 14,000+ Steam releases per year, median lifetime revenue under $5,000, top 1% captures majority of total revenue, power-law distribution applies across all platforms

Success factor analysis: genre selection (some genres have better revenue-to-competition ratios), audience targeting (niche beats broad), timing (avoiding AAA launch windows), and marketing (the primary differentiator between similar-quality games)

Survivorship bias: studying only successful indie games creates a distorted picture. For every Undertale, thousands of equally personal passion projects sold nothing. Analyze failures as rigorously as successes.
The indie advantage: creative freedom, direct player relationship, 100% revenue retention (self-published), rapid iteration, and the ability to serve niches too small for large studios

The indie disadvantage: limited resources, no safety net, marketing expertise gap, platform relationship disadvantage, and the emotional toll of making something deeply personal that may fail commercially
Solo developer vs. small team trade-offs: solo means total creative control but slowest velocity and single points of failure. Teams of 2-5 multiply capability but require coordination overhead.
Financial planning for indie development: calculating runway (months of expenses you can sustain without revenue), opportunity cost of time investment, and minimum viable commercial outcome
Platform economics: Steam takes 30% (dropping to 25% at $10M and 20% at $50M), Epic takes 12%, itch.io lets you choose, consoles require dev kits and certification fees

The overnight success myth: most indie hits were preceded by years of failed projects, community building, and skill development. Hollow Knight's Team Cherry had years of game jam experience before starting development.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Market deep dive: we analyze three indie games released in the past year using SteamDB data: one breakout hit, one moderate success, and one commercial failure. For each, we examine genre positioning, release timing, marketing activity, wishlist counts, review volumes, and estimated revenue. The goal is to identify what differentiated the outcomes beyond game quality, which was comparable across all three.
setup_requirements: SteamDB and SteamSpy access for market research
Game engine of choice installed (Unity, Godot, or Unreal)
Notebook for personal project concept development

review.value: The indie game market follows a power law: the median earns under $5K, the mean is much higher due to outliers. Plan for median, hope for mean. Never plan for outlier success.
For every success story analyzed in GDC talks, thousands of comparable games failed silently. Study failures to understand the full distribution of outcomes.

Calculate your monthly expenses, multiply by expected development months plus 6 months buffer, and that is your minimum required savings or income. Running out of money mid-development is the most common way indie projects die.
Some genres have better ratios of audience demand to competition. Roguelikes are popular but oversaturated. Cozy farming sims have strong demand with fewer competitors. Research before committing.
Hollow Knight was not Team Cherry's first project. They spent years making game jam entries, building skills, and developing their voice. The 'overnight success' had years of invisible preparation.

class_schedule.topic: Scope Management and Minimum Desirable Product
learning_objectives: Apply scope management techniques to keep projects viable for solo developers and small teams
Distinguish between Minimum Viable Product and Minimum Desirable Product for games
Use the MoSCoW method and vertical slice approach for feature prioritization
Create realistic development timelines with built-in buffers for the unexpected
readings: The Art of Game Design by Jesse Schell, Ch. 8-9: Iteration and Scope
Extra Credits: 'Minimum Viable Product in Game Development' (video series)
Rami Ismail, 'How to Scope Small' presentation

lecture_notes.introduction: Scope creep has killed more indie games than bad code, bad art, or bad design combined. The most common trajectory of a failed indie project is: ambitious vision, enthusiastic start, gradual expansion of feature list, realization that the project is too large, loss of motivation, quiet abandonment. The solution is not to dream smaller but to plan smarter. Scope management is the discipline of ruthlessly prioritizing features, cutting what does not serve the core experience, and building a game that can ship in a realistic timeframe with your available resources. The Minimum Desirable Product (MDP) concept, adapted from the tech industry's Minimum Viable Product, defines the smallest version of your game that is genuinely fun and worth the asking price. Not a demo, not a prototype, but a complete game experience that happens to be compact. Many of the most beloved indie games are surprisingly small in scope. Celeste has one mechanic (dash). Undertale is 6-8 hours. Papers, Please has one verb (stamp). What they lack in scope, they make up in depth and polish.
key_concepts: Minimum Desirable Product (MDP): the smallest version of your game that delivers a complete, satisfying experience worth the price. Not minimum viable (barely functional) but minimum desirable (genuinely good, just compact).

MoSCoW prioritization: Must have (core gameplay loop), Should have (quality-of-life features), Could have (extra content and polish), Won't have (features cut entirely from this version). Be ruthless about the Won't category.

The vertical slice approach: build one complete, polished slice of gameplay before expanding horizontally. If this slice is fun with art, audio, and UI, the rest of the game is replication and variation.

Feature budgeting: estimate implementation time for every feature in hours, then multiply by 2-3x for the reality of debugging, polish, and unexpected complications. If the total exceeds your timeline, cut features.
The 80/20 rule for games: 80% of the fun comes from 20% of the features. Identify that 20% and polish it to brilliance. Cut most of the remaining 80% without guilt.

Kill your darlings: features you are emotionally attached to may not serve the game. If playtesting shows a feature is not contributing to the core experience, cut it regardless of how much time you invested.
Timeline planning with buffers: estimate optimistically, then add 50% for unknowns. A 12-month estimate becomes 18 months with buffer. Plan your finances for the buffered timeline.
Scope management tools: Trello, Notion, or GitHub Projects for kanban-style task tracking. Daily standup notes even for solo developers to maintain accountability.
The ship-it mindset: a finished game that exists is infinitely more valuable than a perfect game that does not. Shipping teaches lessons that development never can.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Scope reduction exercise: students present their dream game concept (the one they would make with unlimited time and money). The class then collaboratively reduces the scope to a 6-month MDP by applying MoSCoW prioritization. For each cut feature, we articulate why it is not essential to the core experience. The exercise typically removes 60-70% of features while preserving the game's identity.
setup_requirements: Trello or Notion account for project management
Whiteboard or digital canvas for scope mapping

review.value: An MVP for a game is not desirable: barely functional games get negative reviews. An MDP is complete and satisfying, just compact. Target MDP, not MVP.
Build one polished level before building ten rough ones. If the vertical slice is fun, scale horizontally. If it is not fun, no amount of content will save the game.
Every feature takes 2-3x longer than your initial estimate due to edge cases, debugging, integration testing, and polish. Budget for the multiplied estimate, not the optimistic one.
A finished, shipped game teaches more than any number of abandoned prototypes. The discipline of finishing, even when the game is imperfect, is the most valuable skill an indie developer can develop.

learning_objectives: Build playable prototypes in under 48 hours to validate game concepts before committing to full production
Apply paper prototyping techniques for rapid mechanic validation without code
Use game jams as structured prototyping environments
Evaluate prototypes objectively using playtesting criteria rather than personal attachment
readings: Fullerton, 'Game Design Workshop,' Ch. 7: Prototyping
Ludum Dare and Global Game Jam post-mortems (curated selection on LMS)
GDC Vault: 'Rapid Prototyping: Tips and Tricks'

lecture_notes.introduction: The most expensive way to discover that a game concept does not work is to spend months building it. Rapid prototyping is the practice of testing ideas quickly and cheaply, before investing significant time and resources. A 48-hour prototype can answer the fundamental question: is this core mechanic fun? If the answer is no, you have lost two days instead of six months. If the answer is yes, you have a foundation to build on with confidence. Prototyping is not about building a miniature version of the final game. It is about isolating the core mechanic, the single interaction that the player will repeat thousands of times, and testing whether that interaction is inherently satisfying. Art does not matter. Sound does not matter. Polish does not matter. Only the feel of the core mechanic matters at this stage. This session covers prototyping methodologies from paper prototypes through digital prototypes, the discipline of scoping a prototype to test one specific question, and the difficult skill of evaluating your own work objectively through playtesting.
key_concepts: The core mechanic test: the one interaction the player performs most often must be inherently satisfying. If pressing the jump button does not feel good on its own, no amount of level design will save the game.

Paper prototyping: using index cards, dice, tokens, and a timer to simulate game mechanics without any code. Surprisingly effective for testing strategy games, economy systems, and narrative branching.
Digital prototype scope: one mechanic, one level, one enemy type, one player ability. Placeholder art (colored rectangles). No menus, no save system, no audio. Test the feel, not the features.

48-hour prototype methodology: Day 1 morning: define the one question the prototype answers. Day 1 afternoon-evening: build the core mechanic. Day 2 morning: build one test scenario. Day 2 afternoon: playtest with others. Day 2 evening: evaluate results.
Playtesting methodology: observe silently, do not explain. If the player does not understand without instruction, the design has a communication problem. Record playtest sessions if possible.

Kill criteria: define before building what would cause you to abandon the concept. 'If players do not enjoy the core loop after 5 minutes of playtesting, the concept is not viable.' This prevents sunk-cost attachment.

Prototype evaluation framework: Is the core mechanic inherently fun? Can the player understand it without instruction? Does it support depth (multiple strategies or skill expression)? Can it sustain a full game?
Game jam as prototyping: structured 48-72 hour events that force rapid development and produce playable results. Excellent for testing ideas and building portfolio pieces.
The prototype is not the product: prototypes are disposable. The code is throwaway. The value is the validated (or invalidated) concept, not the codebase.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live rapid prototyping: the class collectively designs a game mechanic in 10 minutes, then the instructor builds a playable digital prototype in 45 minutes while narrating decisions. The prototype uses placeholder art, tests one mechanic in one scenario, and is immediately playtested by students. The class evaluates whether the concept warrants further development using the evaluation framework.
setup_requirements: Game engine with hot reload capability (Godot or Unity)
Index cards, dice, and tokens for paper prototyping

review.value: Define the single question the prototype answers before building it. 'Is this movement mechanic fun?' is a good prototype question. 'Is this game fun?' is too broad.
If the core mechanic is not fun with colored rectangles, it will not be fun with beautiful art. Test the feel before investing in production values.
Define what failure looks like before you build. This prevents emotional attachment from overriding honest evaluation. If playtesters do not enjoy it, accept the data.
Prototypes are disposable experiments, not codebases to build on. The value is the validated concept. Rebuild from scratch for production with proper architecture.

class_schedule.topic: Art on a Budget: Asset Stores, AI Tools, and Style
learning_objectives: Develop a visual style achievable with limited art skills or budget
Evaluate and integrate asset store resources while maintaining visual consistency
Use AI art tools ethically and effectively as part of the production pipeline
Apply art direction principles that maximize visual impact with minimal asset count
readings: Mark Ferrari, 'Effective Pixel Art for Non-Artists' tutorial series
GDC Vault: 'Art Direction for Indie Games on a Budget'
The ethics of AI art in game development: collected perspectives (course reader)

lecture_notes.introduction: Art is the biggest bottleneck for solo indie developers and small teams without dedicated artists. A programmer who can build complex systems may struggle to create a single appealing sprite. But art-related limitations do not have to prevent shipping a game. The indie market has repeatedly proven that stylistic consistency matters more than fidelity, and that creative art direction can make simple visuals feel intentional rather than amateur. This session covers strategies for creating game art with limited skills and budget: choosing art styles that align with your abilities, using asset stores without creating a visual frankenstein, leveraging AI tools as part of the pipeline while navigating ethical considerations, and applying art direction principles that unify disparate elements into a cohesive visual identity. We will study indie games that turned art limitations into distinctive visual identities: Downwell's two-color palette, Thomas Was Alone's colored rectangles, Return of the Obra Dinn's 1-bit rendering, and Undertale's deliberately simple pixel art.
key_concepts: Art style selection based on skill: pixel art (learnable, clear constraints), low-poly 3D (fewer vertices to model), flat/vector 2D (clean lines, scalable), hand-drawn (personality over precision), abstract/geometric (shapes and colors over representation)

The consistency principle: any art style looks intentional when applied consistently. Mixed styles (realistic trees with cartoon characters with pixel-art UI) look amateurish. Pick one style and commit.
Color palette as art direction: limiting to 8-16 colors creates visual cohesion. Use tools like Lospec palettes or Adobe Color to select harmonious palettes that set mood and genre expectations.

Asset store evaluation: Unity Asset Store, itch.io assets, Kenney.nl (free CC0), and OpenGameArt. Selection criteria: consistent art style, compatible resolution/perspective, license terms, and modification potential.
Asset store integration: even good assets rarely work together without modification. Plan to recolor, resize, and modify purchased assets to match your game's palette, perspective, and scale.

AI art tools in game development: Midjourney, Stable Diffusion, and DALL-E as concept art and reference generators. Using AI for texture generation, background art, and prototype visuals. The ethical landscape: training data consent, artist displacement, and disclosure requirements.
The programmer art pipeline: simple shapes with strong animation, color coding for game state (red = danger, green = safe), readable silhouettes, and UI that communicates function over aesthetics
Shader-based visual effects: screen-space effects (CRT filter, color grading, vignette, bloom) can unify disparate art assets under a consistent post-processing style
Commissioning art: when and how to hire freelance artists. Rates ($25-100/hr for 2D, $40-150/hr for 3D), art briefs, milestone-based payment, and managing the client-artist relationship.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Art style transformation: we take a game prototype with placeholder programmer art and apply three different budget art approaches: 1) asset store integration with color palette unification, 2) minimal pixel art created live in Aseprite, and 3) geometric/abstract style with shader-based post-processing. Each approach takes under 30 minutes and demonstrates how limited art investment can dramatically improve visual appeal.
setup_requirements: Aseprite or Piskel for pixel art creation
Free asset packs from Kenney.nl and itch.io
Game project with placeholder art ready for reskinning

review.value: Simple art applied consistently looks professional. Detailed art applied inconsistently looks amateur. Commit to a single style that matches your skills and apply it everywhere.

Limiting to 8-16 colors instantly creates visual cohesion. Many acclaimed indie games use restricted palettes: Downwell (3 colors), Celeste (carefully chosen per-screen palettes), Ape Out (limited warm palette).
AI-generated art raises real ethical concerns about training data consent and artist displacement. Disclose AI usage in your credits and marketing. The legal landscape is evolving; stay informed.
Never use asset store art unmodified. At minimum, apply a unified color palette and post-processing effects. Players recognize stock assets and it signals low effort.

learning_objectives: Create game sound effects using free tools and libraries
Source and license music for indie games within budget constraints
Implement basic audio design principles that enhance game feel
Use procedural and generative audio to create dynamic soundscapes
readings: A Sound Effect Blog: 'Audio for Indie Game Developers on a Budget'
Wwise and FMOD free tier documentation and tutorials
GDC Vault: 'Audio Design on a Shoestring Budget'

lecture_notes.introduction: Audio is the most underappreciated element of game development and the most cost-effective way to elevate a game from amateur to professional. Players often do not consciously notice good audio, but they absolutely notice its absence. A game with competent visuals and excellent audio feels significantly more polished than a game with excellent visuals and no audio. The good news for indie developers is that audio production has never been more accessible. Free sound libraries contain thousands of high-quality effects, free music tools like LMMS and GarageBand can produce professional results, middleware tools like FMOD and Wwise have free indie tiers, and AI-powered audio tools are rapidly expanding what is possible with zero budget. This session covers the complete audio pipeline for indie developers: sourcing and creating sound effects, finding and licensing music, implementing audio in-engine with proper mixing and spatialization, and using adaptive audio techniques that respond to gameplay. We will demonstrate how effective audio design dramatically improves game feel, even when applied to the simplest gameplay mechanics.
key_concepts: Sound effect sources: Freesound.org (CC-licensed library), BFXR/sfxr (procedural retro sound generation), recording your own effects with a smartphone, and purchasing effect packs from professional libraries

Music sourcing options: compose it yourself (LMMS, GarageBand, Reaper), license from royalty-free libraries (Artlist, Epidemic Sound, incompetech.com), commission from freelance composers ($200-2,000 per track), or use AI music tools (Suno, Udio) with appropriate disclosure

The power of juice: audio feedback for every player interaction (jump, land, attack, collect, damage) creates 'game feel.' Celeste's audio design makes every jump, dash, and wall grab feel impactful through precise sound timing.

Audio mixing basics: volume hierarchy (gameplay sounds > music > ambient), frequency separation (low-end for impacts, mid-range for character, high-end for UI), and ducking (lower music during important sound events)
FMOD/Wwise free tier: professional audio middleware with free licenses for indie developers. Real-time mixing, adaptive music, and parameter-driven sound design without complex code.
Adaptive music: music that responds to gameplay state (combat intensity, exploration, danger level). Layered tracks where instruments are added or removed based on game parameters.
Procedural audio: generating sounds algorithmically rather than playing samples. Wind, rain, engine noise, and ambient environments can be generated in real time with infinite variation.

Audio as feedback design: every game state change should have an audio signature. Low health, proximity to danger, successful combo, failed action. Audio communicates information without taking screen space.

Licensing basics: understand the difference between CC0 (no restrictions), CC-BY (attribution required), CC-BY-NC (attribution required, no commercial use), and royalty-free (one-time fee, commercial use permitted)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Audio transformation exercise: we take a simple platformer prototype running in silence and add a complete audio layer in 30 minutes: footsteps, jump, land, collect, damage, ambient background, and a music loop. Before and after comparison demonstrates the transformative impact of audio on perceived game quality. Students see how audio is implemented in Unity using the built-in audio system and basic FMOD integration.
setup_requirements: BFXR for procedural sound effect generation
Freesound.org account for sound library access

review.value: Dollar for dollar, audio is the highest-ROI investment in indie game polish. A few hours of audio work can make a prototype feel professional. Prioritize audio over visual polish if budget is limited.
Every player action should produce audio feedback. Jump, land, attack, collect, damage, menu select. The audio-visual feedback loop is what creates the sensation of direct control.
Music that responds to gameplay (adding intensity during combat, becoming sparse during exploration) costs the same to implement as static music but creates a dramatically more immersive experience.
Always verify the license before using any audio asset. CC-BY-NC cannot be used in a commercial game. Keep a spreadsheet tracking every audio asset, its source, and its license terms.

learning_objectives: Build game awareness with zero marketing budget using organic channels
Create a devlog strategy that builds audience through development transparency
Leverage game development communities for authentic promotion
Measure marketing effectiveness using free analytics tools
readings: Chris Zukowski, 'How to Market a Game' complete blog series
Victoria Tran, 'How to Talk to People About Your Indie Game'
Thomas Brush, 'How I Got 50,000 Wishlists With No Money' (video)

lecture_notes.introduction: Most indie developers have zero marketing budget. No money for ads, no money for PR agencies, no money for booth space at events. The good news is that some of the most successful indie games were marketed entirely through organic channels that cost nothing but time and authenticity. The bad news is that organic marketing is slow, requires consistent effort over months or years, and there are no shortcuts. This session covers the complete zero-budget marketing playbook: devlogging, community engagement, social media content creation, Steam page optimization, and leveraging free promotional opportunities. The core principle is value-first marketing: every piece of content you create should provide genuine value to the viewer, whether that is entertainment, education, or inspiration, with your game as the byproduct rather than the pitch. Developers who share their process authentically build audiences that convert into customers naturally. Developers who broadcast promotional messages into the void build nothing.
key_concepts: Devlog marketing: regular development updates on YouTube, TikTok, Twitter/X, or blog platforms that show the game's evolution. Devlogs build audience by providing entertainment (watching something be created) and education (learning development techniques).

The value-first principle: 80% of your content should provide value independent of your game (entertaining development stories, useful tutorials, interesting design analysis). 20% can be direct promotion.

Steam Coming Soon page as marketing hub: launch the page 6-12 months before release. Optimize tags, description, and screenshots. Every marketing effort should drive traffic to this page for wishlisting.
#ScreenshotSaturday and hashtag participation: weekly participation in game development hashtags on Twitter/X exposes your game to the indie development community and their followers

Reddit strategy for indie games: genuine participation in r/gamedev, r/indiegaming, and genre-specific subreddits. Share development milestones, ask for feedback, and contribute to discussions. The 9:1 ratio of value-to-promotion.
Cross-promotion with other indie developers: featuring other developers' games in your content, participating in bundle events, and building reciprocal relationships that benefit everyone.
Steam Next Fest as free marketing: a week-long event where players try demos. A well-prepared demo with streaming integration can generate thousands of wishlists at zero cost.
Email list building: collecting emails through a game website, offering exclusive content or beta access in exchange. Email has the highest conversion rate of any marketing channel.
Free analytics tools: Steam's built-in traffic analytics, Google Analytics for your website, native social media analytics, and UTM parameters for tracking traffic sources
lecture_notes.demonstration: Marketing audit workshop: each student presents their current marketing presence (or lack thereof) and the class collaboratively creates a 3-month marketing plan using only free channels. We identify the highest-impact actions for each student's situation: for some, it is starting a TikTok devlog. For others, it is launching a Steam Coming Soon page. Each student leaves with a specific weekly action plan.
setup_requirements: Social media accounts on at least two platforms
Google Analytics or equivalent for website tracking

review.value: Content that provides value (entertainment, education, inspiration) builds audience. Content that only promotes your game is ignored. Lead with value, let the game sell itself through genuine interest.
One post per week for 52 weeks builds more audience than one viral post followed by silence. Consistent presence is the foundation of organic marketing.
Your Steam Coming Soon page should be live as early as possible. Every marketing action should drive traffic to this page. Wishlists accumulated over months convert at launch.
An email list converts at 5-15%, far higher than social media (1-3%). Even a small list of 500 engaged subscribers is more valuable than 5,000 passive followers.

learning_objectives: Design a Kickstarter campaign that funds development with realistic goals and timelines
Build a Patreon or ongoing support model for sustained development funding
Calculate funding goals that account for platform fees, taxes, fulfillment, and development costs
Manage backer expectations and communication throughout the development process
readings: Thomas Bidaux, ICO Partners 'Crowdfunding for Games' annual report
Kickstarter Creator Handbook (games category)
Patreon Creator Guide: sustaining creative projects

lecture_notes.introduction: Crowdfunding offers indie developers an alternative to publisher funding and self-financing that also serves as market validation. A successful Kickstarter campaign proves that an audience exists for your game and is willing to pay for it before you invest years in development. A failed campaign is equally valuable information: it tells you that your concept, your presentation, or your audience size is insufficient, saving you from investing years in a game that would not sell. But crowdfunding in the 2020s is fundamentally different from crowdfunding in the 2010s. The gold rush is over. Backers are more skeptical, expectations are higher, and success requires an existing audience. This session covers both Kickstarter (one-time campaigns) and Patreon (ongoing support), examining when each model is appropriate, how to calculate realistic funding goals, how to design reward tiers that do not bankrupt you, and how to maintain backer trust through years of development.
key_concepts: Kickstarter vs. Patreon: Kickstarter funds a specific project with a defined deliverable and timeline. Patreon funds ongoing creative work with regular content. Choose based on whether you need a lump sum or sustained income.

Pre-campaign audience requirement: successful Kickstarter campaigns have 500-2,000 email subscribers or equivalent engaged followers before launch. Without an audience, the campaign fails regardless of quality.

Funding goal calculation: development costs + platform fees (5%) + payment processing (3-5%) + fulfillment costs (if physical rewards) + 15-20% contingency. Underestimating the goal is worse than failing to fund.
Reward tier design: digital-only tiers ($5-$50) have high margins. Physical rewards (art books, figurines) are costly to produce and ship internationally. Most funding comes from the $20-50 range.
Campaign video: 2-3 minutes showing gameplay, team introduction, and campaign value proposition. The first 30 seconds determine whether backers watch or leave.
Stretch goals: planned extensions that maintain momentum as the campaign exceeds its initial goal. Must be achievable without derailing the core project. Each stretch goal adds scope and risk.

Backer communication: monthly updates during development, honest about delays and challenges. Silence is the fastest way to erode backer trust. Transparency about problems earns more respect than false optimism.

Patreon for game development: monthly tiers ($1-$25) offering development access (build access, design input, behind-the-scenes content). Provides stable income but requires consistent content delivery.
Tax and legal considerations: crowdfunding income is taxable. Budget 25-30% for taxes. Fulfillment obligations are legally binding. Consult an accountant before launching.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Campaign design workshop: students design a complete Kickstarter campaign for their game concept. They calculate a funding goal from a realistic budget, design five reward tiers with fulfillment cost analysis, write the campaign description, plan stretch goals, and calculate the break-even backer count. The class evaluates each campaign for feasibility and appeal.
setup_requirements: Kickstarter browsing account for campaign examples
Budget spreadsheet with fulfillment cost calculator

review.value: Crowdfunding converts existing interest into funding. It does not create interest. Build your audience for months before launching the campaign.
A $50 tier with a physical art book costs $10-15 to print, $5-15 to ship internationally, plus platform and processing fees. Your actual margin may be only $15-20. Calculate before offering.
Every stretch goal adds scope. An 8-month project with 10 stretch goals becomes a 16-month project. Be honest about what each goal adds to the timeline.
Monthly updates minimum. Backers who funded your game deserve to see progress. Silence between updates breeds anxiety, speculation, and negative word-of-mouth.

learning_objectives: Evaluate whether Early Access is appropriate for a specific game and development situation
Plan an Early Access launch with appropriate content, pricing, and communication
Manage the transition from Early Access to full release with maintained momentum
Handle community feedback during Early Access without losing design vision
readings: Steamworks: Early Access Documentation and Best Practices
GDC Vault: 'Early Access Done Right: Lessons from Hades, Slay the Spire, and Valheim'
Lars Doucet, 'What I Learned Selling an Early Access Game'

lecture_notes.introduction: Early Access, selling a game in an unfinished state at a discount with the promise of ongoing development, has become one of the most powerful strategies available to indie developers. Some of the most successful indie games of the past decade launched through Early Access: Hades, Slay the Spire, Valheim, Factorio, and Baldur's Gate 3. When done well, Early Access provides revenue to fund continued development, a community of invested players who provide feedback and testing, and a marketing narrative of visible improvement that generates ongoing coverage. When done poorly, Early Access burns out the player base before the game is ready, attracts negative reviews that permanently damage the store page, and commits the developer to a public development timeline they cannot maintain. This session covers the strategic decision of whether to use Early Access, how to prepare for a successful EA launch, how to manage the EA period, and how to execute the transition to 1.0 with renewed momentum.
key_concepts: When Early Access works: games with systemic depth that benefit from iteration (roguelikes, simulation, sandbox, survival), games where community feedback meaningfully improves design, and developers who can sustain a regular update cadence.

When Early Access fails: narrative-driven games where early exposure spoils the experience, games where the core loop is not yet fun (EA players will not wait for it to become fun), and developers who cannot commit to a consistent update schedule.

EA launch readiness: the game at EA launch must already be fun. It can be content-incomplete but mechanically solid. Minimum: the core gameplay loop is enjoyable for 5-10 hours, the game is stable (no frequent crashes), and the roadmap is honest.
Pricing strategy: EA price should be 10-20% below planned 1.0 price. Early buyers get a discount for taking a risk on an unfinished product. Price increases at 1.0 are common and accepted.
Update cadence: regular, predictable updates (every 2-4 weeks for content, weekly for bug fixes). Hades shipped 66 updates during its 2-year EA period. Consistency builds trust.

Community feedback management: listen to everything, act on the data, not individual voices. Feedback that aligns with your vision is a priority. Feedback that contradicts your vision requires careful evaluation.

The loud minority problem: the most vocal community members are not representative. A feature 10 people complain about loudly may be loved by 10,000 silent players. Use data, not volume, to guide decisions.
1.0 transition: the full release must feel like an event. New trailer, new marketing push, significant content addition, and if possible, a launch sale for the 1.0 version to reach new audiences.
Review management during EA: Steam separates EA reviews from 1.0 reviews. Negative EA reviews for 'lack of content' will not disappear but will be contextualized. Aim for 80%+ positive during EA.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Early Access roadmap exercise: students plan a 12-month Early Access roadmap for their game concept. They define the EA launch content (minimum viable fun), the update schedule (content and bug fix cadence), 4-6 major milestones, the 1.0 feature set, and the 1.0 launch marketing strategy. The class evaluates each roadmap for realism and sustainability.
setup_requirements: Case studies of successful and failed EA games

review.value: The game must already be fun at EA launch. Players will not wait for it to become fun. Content can be incomplete but the core loop must be enjoyable for several hours.
Regular updates (every 2-4 weeks) maintain community engagement and demonstrate commitment. Long gaps between updates cause player churn and negative sentiment.
Ten loud forum posts do not override ten thousand quiet play sessions. Use analytics data (play time, feature engagement, drop-off points) to guide development priorities, not volume of complaints.
The 1.0 release is a second marketing moment. New trailer, press outreach, influencer keys, and a launch discount. Treat it as a full launch, not an update.

learning_objectives: Build structured feedback channels that collect actionable data from players
Distinguish between feedback that improves the game and feedback that changes the game into something else
Implement beta testing programs that generate useful design insights
Communicate development decisions transparently to maintain community trust
readings: GDC Vault: 'Listening to Your Players Without Losing Your Vision'
Victoria Tran, 'Community-Driven Development Done Right'
Supergiant Games blog: How Hades was shaped by Early Access feedback

lecture_notes.introduction: Community feedback is simultaneously the most valuable resource and the most dangerous trap available to an indie developer. At its best, player feedback reveals design blind spots, identifies bugs and frustrations that the developer cannot see from the inside, and generates ideas that improve the game in ways the developer never imagined. At its worst, player feedback pulls the game in contradictory directions, demands features that do not serve the core experience, and creates a vocal minority whose preferences override the silent majority's enjoyment. The skill of community feedback integration is not just listening; it is filtering. Knowing what feedback to act on, what to acknowledge and set aside, and what to politely decline is essential to maintaining design vision while benefiting from community insight. This session covers structured feedback collection, analysis methodologies, beta testing programs, and communication strategies that maintain trust even when you do not implement what players ask for.
key_concepts: Feedback channel design: separate channels for bug reports (structured forms with reproduction steps), feature requests (voteable), balance feedback (data-supported), and general discussion (unstructured).

Feedback categorization: bugs (objective, fix them), usability issues (if players consistently misunderstand something, the design has a communication problem), balance concerns (validate with data before acting), feature requests (evaluate against vision and scope), and taste preferences (acknowledge but do not act unless they reveal a deeper issue).

The vision filter: every piece of feedback should be evaluated against the game's core design vision. 'Make the game easier' is feedback. Whether you act on it depends on whether your vision is an accessible experience or a challenging one.
Beta testing program design: closed beta with 50-200 players, structured feedback forms, play session logging, and weekly surveys. Betas test the experience, not just the code.

Quantitative vs. qualitative feedback: player behavior data (where do they die? where do they quit? what do they skip?) is more reliable than stated preferences. Players often misidentify the source of their frustration.

The 'ask why' technique: when a player says 'the game needs X feature,' ask why. The underlying need ('I want to feel more in control of my build') can often be addressed with a simpler solution than the proposed feature.

Transparent communication about feedback: share what feedback you received, what you are acting on, what you are not (and why), and how player input influenced specific decisions. This builds trust even when you decline suggestions.

The Developer Update format: regular posts summarizing what changed, what is being worked on, what feedback influenced decisions, and what is coming next. This format keeps the community informed and invested.

Avoiding design-by-committee: the game must have a creative director (even if it is a solo developer). One person must have final authority over design decisions, informed by feedback but not dictated by it.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Feedback analysis exercise: students receive a dataset of 200 player feedback comments from a fictional game's beta test. They categorize each comment, identify the top 5 actionable insights, propose changes that address the underlying needs without necessarily implementing the requested features, and draft a Developer Update communicating their decisions. The class compares their analyses and discusses where they agree and disagree.
setup_requirements: Feedback dataset (200 comments, provided on LMS)

review.value: Feedback is input, not instruction. Every suggestion is evaluated against the game's design vision. 'Make it easier' is valid feedback for a casual game and invalid for a Souls-like.

What players do is more reliable than what they say. If players say the boss is too hard but the death rate is only 40%, the boss is fine. If the death rate is 95%, it is too hard regardless of what anyone says.
Players identify symptoms, not causes. 'Add a minimap' might actually mean 'I keep getting lost.' The solution might be better level design landmarks, not a minimap.
Regular updates showing how feedback influenced decisions build trust even when you decline suggestions. Players who feel heard are more patient than players who feel ignored.

learning_objectives: Navigate Steam's publishing process from Steamworks application through store page launch
Use itch.io as a platform for demos, game jam entries, and niche games
Compare self-publishing economics across Steam, itch.io, Epic Games Store, and GOG
Optimize store pages for discoverability on each platform
readings: Steamworks Developer Documentation: Getting Started
itch.io Creator Documentation and Revenue Sharing Model
Chris Zukowski, 'Which Platform Should You Release Your Indie Game On?'

lecture_notes.introduction: Self-publishing an indie game is more accessible than ever, but each platform has its own requirements, economics, and audience. Steam dominates PC gaming with over 30 million daily active users and handles the majority of indie game revenue. Itch.io offers a developer-friendly alternative with flexible revenue sharing and no curation barrier. Epic Games Store, GOG, and console platforms offer additional reach but with different requirements. This session provides a practical walkthrough of the publishing process on each major platform, comparing the economics, audience, discoverability, and requirements of each. We will focus primarily on Steam as the dominant indie platform, covering the Steamworks onboarding process, store page optimization, pricing strategy, and the Steam algorithm's impact on discoverability. The goal is to demystify the publishing process so that when your game is ready, the business side does not delay your launch.
key_concepts: Steamworks onboarding: $100 application fee per game, identity verification, tax information, and account setup. The process takes 1-2 weeks. Build IDs and store page assets must be prepared in advance.

Steam store page optimization: capsule images at multiple resolutions, 5-8 screenshots showing gameplay (not menus), short description (300 characters, most important), long description with headers and formatting, and tag selection for algorithmic matching.
Steam pricing strategy: Steamworks provides regional pricing recommendations. Launch discount (10-15%) is standard. Price must support the perception of value while covering development costs.
Steam algorithm basics: discovery queue matching based on tags and user behavior, new release visibility boost for 2-3 weeks, and wishlist-to-sale conversion as a key metric the algorithm watches.

Itch.io advantages: no curation barrier (anyone can publish), choose-your-own revenue share (even 100% to developer), strong community for experimental and niche games, game jam hosting, and web-playable games.
Itch.io limitations: much smaller audience than Steam, limited discoverability tools, no achievement or community features equivalent to Steam, and lower average revenue per game.
Epic Games Store indie publishing: lower revenue share (12% vs. Steam's 30%), but smaller player base and less organic discoverability. Grants program offers funding for exclusive titles.
GOG: DRM-free platform with curated selection. Smaller audience but highly engaged and willing to pay. Must pass editorial review to be listed.
Multi-platform release strategy: launch on Steam first (largest audience), add itch.io for DRM-free option, and evaluate Epic/GOG based on genre fit and audience overlap.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Steamworks walkthrough: live tour of the Steamworks developer portal, showing the store page editor, build upload process, achievement configuration, and analytics dashboard. Students see exactly what the publishing interface looks like and what assets need to be prepared before launch. We also create a quick itch.io game page for comparison, demonstrating the different onboarding experience.
setup_requirements: Itch.io account for page creation exercise
Store page asset templates (capsule images, screenshots)

review.value: Steam generates 85-95% of indie PC game revenue. Other platforms are supplementary. Optimize for Steam first, then expand to other platforms based on genre fit.
Your store page converts visitors to wishlists/purchases. Capsule image (0.3 second impression), first two screenshots (5 seconds), and short description (15 seconds) determine conversion.
Itch.io is the home of game jams. Publishing game jam entries on itch.io builds portfolio, community recognition, and practice with the shipping process.
Steam takes 30% but has the largest audience. Itch.io lets you keep up to 100% but has far fewer buyers. Epic takes 12% but has limited organic discoverability. Revenue equals volume times margin.

learning_objectives: Participate effectively in game jams as creative exercises and portfolio builders
Apply game jam time management to produce complete, playable games in 48-72 hours
Evaluate game jam entries for commercial viability and prototype potential
Build community connections through jam participation and collaboration
readings: Ludum Dare and Global Game Jam participation guides
GDC Vault: 'From Game Jam to Shipped Product: Lessons and Pitfalls'
Case studies: games that grew from jam entries (Celeste, Superhot, Untitled Goose Game)

lecture_notes.introduction: Game jams are concentrated bursts of game development where participants create complete games within strict time constraints, typically 48 to 72 hours. They are simultaneously the best training ground for indie developers and a prolific source of commercial game ideas. Celeste began as a PICO-8 game jam entry. Superhot was born in a 7-day game jam. Untitled Goose Game started as a joke in a jam brainstorming session. Game jams teach skills that formal education often misses: ruthless scope management, rapid decision-making, finishing under pressure, and the willingness to ship imperfect work. They also provide portfolio pieces, community connections, and a regular cadence of creative experimentation that keeps skills sharp during long development projects. This session covers jam participation strategy: how to choose jams, how to manage time during a jam, how to evaluate whether a jam entry has commercial potential, and how to cultivate the jam mindset as a ongoing creative practice.
key_concepts: Major game jams: Ludum Dare (48/72 hours, online, largest), Global Game Jam (48 hours, local sites worldwide), GMTK Game Jam (48 hours, design-focused), and platform-specific jams (PICO-8, Godot, etc.)

Time management in a 48-hour jam: Hours 0-4: brainstorm and commit to a concept. Hours 4-16: build the core mechanic. Hours 16-24: build the content (level, progression). Hours 24-36: polish (audio, juice, UI). Hours 36-48: fix bugs, build, and submit. Sleep: at least 6-8 hours total.
Scope for jams: one mechanic, one level or challenge sequence, 5-10 minutes of play. Art should be simple but consistent. Audio matters more than visuals for game feel.
The jam theme as constraint: themes force creative thinking. The best jam games interpret themes metaphorically or unexpectedly rather than literally.
Solo vs. team jams: solo jams test self-sufficiency. Team jams test collaboration and communication under pressure. Both are valuable experiences.

Jam-to-commercial pipeline: after a jam, evaluate the entry honestly. If playtest feedback is enthusiastic and the mechanic has depth potential, the concept may warrant full development. But the jam code should be rewritten, not expanded.

Rating and feedback culture: jam communities provide constructive feedback. Rate other entries genuinely, leave helpful comments, and engage with the community. This builds relationships and reputation.
The regular jam practice: participating in 2-4 jams per year keeps prototyping skills sharp, generates portfolio pieces, and provides creative variety during long development projects.
Famous jam-to-game examples: Celeste (PICO-8 jam -> full game, 500K+ copies), Superhot (7DFPS jam -> full game, 2M+ copies), Katamari Damacy (student project/jam -> commercial classic)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mini-jam exercise: the class has 90 minutes to build a complete game responding to a theme drawn at random. Working individually, students must produce a playable game with at least one mechanic, one level, and one win/lose condition. The remaining 90 minutes are spent playtesting each entry, providing feedback, and evaluating commercial viability. This compressed format simulates the jam experience.
setup_requirements: Game engine preinstalled with project template
Timer displayed prominently for time management

review.value: The most important skill in a jam is knowing when to cut features. If the core mechanic is not working by hour 16, pivot or simplify. Do not spend 40 hours on something that will not be fun.
A finished jam game, no matter how simple, teaches more than an ambitious unfinished one. The discipline of shipping under time pressure is the most valuable jam skill.
If a jam entry shows commercial potential, rewrite the code from scratch with proper architecture. Jam code is held together by duct tape and caffeine. It is not a codebase to build on.
Play and rate at least 20 other entries per jam. Leave constructive comments. The relationships built through jam communities lead to collaborations, testing partnerships, and mutual promotion.

learning_objectives: Conduct a structured post-mortem analysis of a completed game project
Identify patterns in what went right and what went wrong to inform future projects
Learn from published post-mortems of successful and failed indie games
Develop the habit of reflective practice as a continuous improvement tool
readings: Gamasutra/Game Developer: Post-mortem archive (curated selection on LMS)
Celeste post-mortem by Matt Thorson (GDC Vault)
Stardew Valley post-mortem by ConcernedApe (various interviews compiled)

lecture_notes.introduction: The post-mortem is one of the most valuable tools in a game developer's practice, and one of the most under-utilized. A post-mortem is a structured analysis of a completed project: what went right, what went wrong, and what you would do differently. The exercise forces honesty about decisions and outcomes that is difficult to achieve during active development when emotions and sunk costs cloud judgment. The games industry has a rich tradition of published post-mortems, originally in Game Developer Magazine and now on Gamasutra and at GDC. These documents are invaluable because they reveal the messy reality behind polished final products. Every game, no matter how successful, had moments of crisis, failed features, scope cuts, and difficult decisions. Reading and writing post-mortems builds pattern recognition: certain mistakes recur across nearly every project, and recognizing them early is the difference between a manageable problem and a project-ending one.
key_concepts: Post-mortem structure: 5 things that went right, 5 things that went wrong, and for each, the specific circumstances, the impact on the project, and the lesson for future work.

Common 'went right' patterns: clear design vision from the start, early prototyping that validated the concept, regular playtesting that caught problems early, scope management that kept the project finishable.
Common 'went wrong' patterns: scope creep, underestimated production time (especially for content), technical debt accumulated during prototyping, marketing started too late, burnout from overwork.

Honest assessment: the hardest part of a post-mortem is being honest about your own mistakes. Blaming external factors (the market, the platform, bad luck) prevents learning. Focus on what was within your control.

Quantitative post-mortem data: planned vs. actual timeline, planned vs. actual budget, feature-complete list vs. original design document, marketing metrics (wishlists, conversion, revenue), and review analysis.
Published post-mortems as learning material: reading 10-20 post-mortems from games similar to yours provides better project planning intelligence than any textbook.
Post-mortem as portfolio piece: a well-written post-mortem demonstrates professional maturity, analytical thinking, and the ability to learn from experience. Employers value this highly.
The retrospective habit: do not wait for the project to end. Conduct mini-retrospectives at milestones (every 3 months or at major feature completions) to course-correct during development.

Team post-mortems: when working with others, ensure every team member contributes their perspective. Different roles see different problems. The programmer's 'went wrong' may be different from the designer's.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Published post-mortem analysis: the class reads two post-mortems together, one from a successful indie game (Hollow Knight) and one from a commercial failure. We identify the patterns: where did decisions diverge? What did the successful team do differently? We then conduct a structured post-mortem of the class's game jam entries from Week 11, practicing the format with a real recent experience.
setup_requirements: Game jam entries from Week 11 for fresh analysis

review.value: A post-mortem that does not contain uncomfortable truths is not a post-mortem. 'What went wrong' must include decisions you made, not just circumstances beyond your control.

Scope creep, late marketing, underestimated content production time, and technical debt appear in the majority of post-mortems. If your project has any of these, you are not uniquely unlucky; you are experiencing the default.
Do not wait for the end to reflect. A quarterly retrospective during development catches problems while they are still fixable. The post-mortem at the end captures lessons for the next project.
A thoughtful post-mortem published on your blog or portfolio demonstrates professional maturity. Employers prefer candidates who can analyze their own work critically.

learning_objectives: Recognize the signs of burnout in yourself and team members
Implement sustainable development practices that protect long-term productivity
Plan work schedules and milestones that prevent crunch
Build support systems and habits that sustain creative output over multi-year projects
readings: Jason Schreier, 'Press Reset: Ruin and Recovery in the Video Game Industry'
IGDA Quality of Life survey findings
Rami Ismail, 'Mental Health and Indie Development' talk

lecture_notes.introduction: Indie game development is a marathon, not a sprint, and the burnout rate is devastating. Studies suggest that over 50% of indie projects are abandoned before completion, and developer mental health is a primary factor. The combination of financial pressure, creative doubt, isolation (especially for solo developers), and the relentless comparison to successful peers creates conditions that are psychologically dangerous. The game industry's broader culture of crunch, where 60-80 hour weeks are normalized, has been extensively documented and criticized, but indie developers often impose crunch on themselves, believing that passion justifies self-exploitation. This session confronts the sustainability challenge directly. We will examine the research on burnout, the specific risk factors for indie developers, and the practical strategies that allow developers to sustain creative output over multi-year projects without destroying their health and relationships. Sustainability is not a luxury; it is a prerequisite for finishing a game.
key_concepts: Burnout symptoms: persistent exhaustion that does not improve with rest, loss of enthusiasm for the project, cynicism about the game's prospects, reduced productivity despite increased hours, and physical symptoms (insomnia, headaches, digestive issues).

Indie-specific burnout factors: financial uncertainty (runway anxiety), social isolation (working alone for months), scope overwhelm (the project feels impossible to finish), comparison trap (other developers seem more successful), and identity fusion (your self-worth becomes tied to the game's success).

Sustainable hours: research consistently shows that productivity per hour drops significantly after 40 hours per week. After 55 hours, total output actually decreases due to errors, poor decisions, and rework. Crunch produces less, not more.

The rhythm of deep work: 4-6 hours of focused creative work per day is the sustainable maximum for most people. Schedule deep work in the morning, administrative and communication tasks in the afternoon.
Milestone-based scheduling: set bi-weekly or monthly milestones that are achievable without overtime. If you regularly miss milestones, the scope is too large, not the effort too small.
The 'day off' non-negotiable: at least one full day per week with no game development. This is not laziness; it is maintenance. Creativity requires rest and varied input to function.
Support systems: a mentor or advisor (even an online community), a peer accountability partner, regular contact with people outside the games industry, and professional mental health support.

Financial sustainability models: maintaining part-time income during development, applying for grants (Indie Fund, Unreal MegaGrants, platform holder grants), and setting a hard financial deadline for project viability assessment.

The identity separation: you are not your game. Your game can fail and you can still be a successful, valued person. This separation is essential for maintaining perspective during difficult development periods.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Sustainability planning exercise: each student creates a personal sustainability plan for their game project. They define their maximum weekly hours, schedule rest days, identify their burnout warning signs, plan financial checkpoints, and list support contacts. The class discusses these plans openly, normalizing the conversation about mental health and sustainable practices in game development.
setup_requirements: Weekly schedule template with blocked rest time
Resource list for mental health support in the game industry

review.value: Productivity per hour drops after 40 hours and total output drops after 55 hours. Working more does not produce more. Plan for 40 hours of focused work and protect that boundary.
Days off are not lost development time. They are investments in sustained productivity. Burnout recovery takes weeks or months. Prevention takes one day per week.
Set quarterly financial check-ins: can you sustain development for the next 6 months? If not, adjust scope, seek funding, or take contract work. Running out of money mid-project is the worst outcome.
Your game is something you made, not who you are. A game that fails commercially does not make you a failure. This separation is essential for surviving the inevitable setbacks.

class_schedule.topic: Business and Legal Basics for Indies
learning_objectives: Understand the basic legal structures available for indie game studios (sole proprietorship, LLC, corporation)
Protect intellectual property through copyright, trademark, and trade secret basics
Navigate contract essentials for freelancers, collaborators, and publishers
Manage basic business operations: invoicing, taxes, and record-keeping
readings: Tom Buscaglia, 'The Game Attorney': legal basics for indie developers
IGDA Legal Resources for Game Developers
S. Gregory Boyd, 'Video Game Law' selected chapters

lecture_notes.introduction: Indie game development is a business, and treating it as purely a creative pursuit without business foundations creates serious risks. Without a business entity, your personal assets are exposed to liability. Without contracts, collaborator relationships can dissolve into disputes over ownership. Without basic IP protection, your game's name, characters, and code have weaker legal standing. Without financial record-keeping, tax season becomes a nightmare. This session covers the business and legal fundamentals that every indie developer needs: not the depth that would replace legal counsel, but the literacy needed to make informed decisions and ask the right questions. We cover business entity formation, intellectual property basics, contract essentials for common indie relationships (collaborators, freelancers, publishers, voice actors), and basic financial management. The goal is not to turn you into a lawyer or accountant, but to ensure you do not make avoidable mistakes that cost more to fix later.
key_concepts: Business entity options: sole proprietorship (simplest, no liability protection), LLC (liability protection, pass-through taxation, recommended for most indie studios), S-Corp (tax advantages at higher income levels, more complexity).
Liability protection: an LLC separates personal assets from business liabilities. If your game is sued (unlikely but possible), your personal savings and property are protected.

Copyright basics: your code, art, music, and game design documents are automatically copyrighted upon creation. Registration with the Copyright Office ($65) provides additional legal protection and damages in infringement cases.
Trademark basics: your game's name and logo can be trademarked to prevent others from using confusingly similar names. Search the USPTO database before committing to a game name.

Collaboration agreements: before writing a single line of code with anyone, agree in writing on: ownership of IP created, revenue sharing, decision-making authority, and what happens if someone leaves.

Work-for-hire contracts for freelancers: when hiring artists, musicians, or voice actors, ensure the contract specifies that the work is 'work for hire' and that all IP rights transfer to you. Without this, the freelancer may own their contribution.

Revenue sharing agreements: if collaborating with revenue share instead of upfront payment, document the percentage, when payments begin, what revenue is included (gross vs. net), and how long the agreement lasts.

Basic tax obligations: game development income is taxable. Estimated quarterly tax payments, deductible business expenses (hardware, software, conference travel), and the importance of keeping receipts and records.

When to hire a lawyer: entity formation, any publishing deal, any contract over $5,000, and any dispute. Legal fees ($150-400/hr) are an investment, not an expense. A bad contract can cost orders of magnitude more.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Contract review exercise: students receive three sample contracts (a freelance art agreement, a collaboration agreement, and a simplified publishing deal) and identify the key terms, potential issues, and missing protections in each. The class discusses what they would negotiate for and what red flags they found. This exercise builds contract literacy through practical application.
setup_requirements: LLC formation guide for the student's state/country

review.value: Form an LLC before generating any revenue. The liability protection alone is worth the $100-500 filing fee. Without one, a lawsuit targets your personal assets.
Never collaborate without a written agreement, even with friends. Especially with friends. Verbal agreements are nearly impossible to enforce and friendships do not survive ownership disputes.
Every freelancer contract must include a work-for-hire clause transferring IP rights to you. Without it, the artist technically owns the character designs they created for your game.
Before naming your game, search the USPTO trademark database and Steam. Discovering a trademark conflict after building brand recognition is expensive and demoralizing.

learning_objectives: Present a comprehensive indie game pitch demonstrating mastery of all course concepts
Defend scope, market positioning, business strategy, and development plan under questioning
Evaluate peer pitches using professional criteria for concept viability, feasibility, and presentation quality
Synthesize creative vision with market reality into an actionable development plan
readings: Review all course materials and prepare final pitch presentation
Pitch deck best practices (curated examples on LMS)
Peer evaluation rubric (provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: This final session brings together every concept from the course into a single deliverable: a comprehensive indie game pitch. Each student presents a 15-minute pitch for an indie game they intend to build, covering concept, market analysis, scope management, art and audio strategy, marketing plan, publishing strategy, financial plan, and development timeline. The pitch is evaluated not just on the quality of the game concept but on the feasibility and completeness of the plan to bring it to market. A brilliant game concept with no marketing plan, unrealistic scope, or insufficient financial runway is not a viable project. Conversely, a modest concept with excellent market positioning, achievable scope, and a solid business plan is a strong bet. The pitch format simulates presenting to a publisher, grant committee, or investor: you must be concise, persuasive, and prepared for challenging questions about your assumptions and decisions.
key_concepts: Pitch structure: hook (30 seconds), game concept (2 minutes), market analysis (2 minutes), scope and timeline (2 minutes), art/audio strategy (2 minutes), marketing plan (2 minutes), business and financial plan (2 minutes), Q&A (3 minutes).
The hook: what makes this game unique in one sentence? If you cannot articulate the unique value proposition in 30 seconds, the concept needs sharpening.

Market analysis credibility: show you have researched the competitive landscape with specific competitor titles, estimated revenue, and market gap analysis. Vague claims like 'the market is huge' undermine credibility.
Scope realism: the timeline must be achievable with the stated team size and resources. Show the MoSCoW prioritization, the vertical slice plan, and buffer time for unknowns.
Financial viability: development cost estimate, funding source (savings, crowdfunding, publisher, grants), monthly burn rate, runway in months, and minimum sales target for viability.
Risk assessment: the three biggest risks to the project (market, technical, personal) and the mitigation strategy for each. Acknowledging risks demonstrates maturity, not weakness.
Presentation skills: make eye contact, speak clearly, lead with enthusiasm, support with data. The pitch must convey both passion for the game and rational planning for the business.
Peer evaluation criteria: concept originality, market viability, scope realism, plan completeness, presentation quality, and Q&A performance.
lecture_notes.demonstration: The instructor delivers a sample pitch first, intentionally including one weak section, and the class identifies the weakness and suggests improvements. This calibrates the evaluation standard and helps students improve their own pitches before presenting.

review.value: If you cannot explain what makes your game unique in one sentence, refine the concept until you can. 'A Metroidvania set in a procedurally generated coral reef' is clear. 'An innovative action-adventure experience' is not.
Scope, timeline, and team size are a triangle. You can choose two. A solo developer with a 12-month timeline must have a small scope. Claiming otherwise destroys pitch credibility.
Presenting a plan with no identified risks signals naivety, not confidence. Naming your three biggest risks and your mitigation strategies demonstrates professional maturity.
At this stage, the game does not exist yet. The plan is the product being evaluated. A great concept with a poor plan is a weaker pitch than a good concept with an excellent plan.

quizzes.topic: Market Reality, Scope Management, and Prototyping

questions.question: An indie developer plans to build a 40-hour open-world RPG as a solo project with a 12-month timeline and no prior shipped games. Apply MoSCoW prioritization to reduce this to a viable Minimum Desirable Product. Describe what stays, what gets cut, and what the resulting game looks like. Justify each major cut.
questions.answer: Must have: one core gameplay loop (combat + dialogue), one contained area (a village and its surroundings, not an open world), one story arc (2-4 hours), 3-5 unique enemy types, and a complete beginning-middle-end structure. Should have: a crafting or upgrade system (simplified), side quests (2-3 optional), and an NPC relationship system (basic). Could have: day/night cycle, additional areas beyond the village, and voice acting. Won't have: open world exploration (replaced with contained hub), 40 hours of content (replaced with 3-5 polished hours), procedural content generation, multiplayer, and console ports. Resulting game: a focused narrative RPG set in a single richly detailed village with a 3-5 hour story, tight combat, and meaningful NPC interactions. Think 'Coffee Talk meets basic JRPG combat' rather than 'solo Skyrim.' Justification: open-world content production requires hundreds of hours of level design alone. Reducing to a contained area makes the project feasible for one person in 12 months while preserving the RPG experience.

rubric.key: MoSCoW categories are correctly applied with specific features in each
Open world correctly identified as the primary scope cut with justification
Resulting MDP is a coherent game, not just a list of remaining features
Time estimate is realistic for a solo developer with the reduced scope

questions.question: You built a 48-hour prototype and it tested poorly: players found the core mechanic confusing and did not enjoy the loop after 5 minutes. But you spent significant time on the concept art, wrote a detailed design document, and are emotionally invested. Using the principles from this course, describe the decision you should make and the reasoning process behind it.
questions.answer: You should strongly consider abandoning the concept and moving to the next idea. Reasoning: the prototype was specifically designed to test whether the core mechanic is fun. The playtest answer was no. The sunk cost of concept art and design documents is irrelevant to the mechanic's viability. The kill criteria defined before building (players should enjoy the core loop after 5 minutes) was not met. However, before final abandonment: review playtest notes for patterns. If all players were confused by the same thing, the problem might be communication rather than the mechanic itself. Try one iteration that simplifies or clarifies the mechanic. If the second test still fails, abandon with confidence. The concept art can be reused. The design document skills transfer. The real cost is opportunity cost: every month spent on a mechanic players do not enjoy is a month not spent on one they would.

rubric.key: Recommends abandonment based on playtest data, not doubling down
Identifies sunk cost fallacy and addresses it explicitly
Allows for one refinement iteration before final decision
Frames the cost in terms of opportunity cost of continued investment

questions.question: Calculate the financial runway for a solo developer with $30,000 in savings, $2,000/month living expenses, who plans to develop a game over 12 months. Include the 50% buffer rule. Then calculate the minimum first-year revenue needed to justify the investment (including opportunity cost of a $60,000/year salary).
questions.answer: Runway calculation: $2,000/month * 12 months = $24,000 minimum. With 50% buffer: $24,000 * 1.5 = $36,000 needed. Available: $30,000. The developer is $6,000 short of a fully buffered runway and will run out of money at month 15 (but the project takes 18 months with buffer). Recommendation: either reduce scope to 8-9 months, maintain part-time income, or save an additional $6,000 before starting. Minimum revenue calculation: Direct development cost: $24,000 (12 months * $2,000). Opportunity cost: $60,000 (one year of salary foregone). Total investment: $84,000. At Steam's 70% developer share after the 30% platform fee, minimum gross revenue needed: $84,000 / 0.7 = $120,000 gross Steam revenue. At $15 per copy, that is 8,000 copies. Using the Boxleiter method in reverse: 8,000 copies requires approximately 150-400 reviews (at 20-60x multiplier), which places the game well above median but achievable for a well-marketed indie game.

rubric.key: Correct runway calculation with buffer showing the shortfall
Practical recommendation to address the shortfall
Revenue calculation includes opportunity cost and platform fees
Converts revenue target to unit sales and validates against market data

questions.question: A programmer-developer with no art skills is building a 2D action platformer. Propose three different art style strategies they could use, ranked by time investment. For each, specify the tools, estimated learning curve, and one successful indie game that used a similar approach.
questions.answer: Strategy 1 (lowest time investment): Geometric/abstract style using colored shapes and shader effects. Tools: game engine's built-in shape rendering plus post-processing (CRT filter, bloom, color grading). Learning curve: 1-2 weeks for shader basics. Example: Thomas Was Alone (colored rectangles with strong lighting and narration). Strategy 2 (moderate time investment): Pixel art with limited palette. Tools: Aseprite ($20) with Lospec palette constraints (8-16 colors). Learning curve: 2-4 weeks for basic sprite animation at 16x16 or 32x32 resolution. Example: Downwell (3-color palette, simple sprites, strong visual identity). Strategy 3 (highest time investment but high return): Asset store integration with unified palette and post-processing. Tools: Kenney.nl free assets or purchased asset packs, modified with consistent color palette in Photoshop/GIMP, unified by screen-space shader effects. Learning curve: 1-2 weeks for asset modification workflow. Example: many successful indie games use modified asset store art without disclosure; Vampire Survivors uses intentionally simple modified assets.

rubric.key: Three distinct strategies ranked by time investment
Specific tools and realistic learning curves for each
Real game examples that validate each approach
All strategies are feasible for someone with no art skills

questions.question: Explain why audio has the highest return on investment for indie game polish. Describe the minimum audio layer a game needs and estimate the time to implement it using free tools.
questions.answer: Audio has the highest ROI because: players subconsciously evaluate game quality partly through audio fidelity, audio implementation is faster than visual polish for comparable impact, free high-quality assets exist in large quantities (Freesound.org), and audio feedback is essential for game feel (every action without audio feedback feels disconnected). Minimum audio layer: player movement sounds (footsteps, jump, land), player action sounds (attack, interact), feedback sounds (damage taken, item collected, UI click), ambient background loop, and one music track. Time estimate: 2-4 hours to source all effects from Freesound.org and BFXR, 2-3 hours to implement in-engine with basic mixing (gameplay sounds louder than music, spatial attenuation for distance), and 1-2 hours for a music loop (license from incompetech.com or Artlist). Total: 5-9 hours for a complete basic audio layer. By comparison, creating equivalent visual polish (animations, particles, lighting) would take 20-40 hours.

rubric.key: ROI reasoning is specific and compelling
Minimum audio layer covers actions, feedback, ambient, and music
Time estimate is realistic for free tools with specific sources named
Comparison to visual polish time demonstrates the ROI difference

questions.question: You are hiring a freelance 2D artist to create 20 character sprites for your indie game. Describe the essential contract terms you must include and explain what goes wrong without each term.
questions.answer: Essential term 1: Work-for-hire clause with full IP transfer. Without it, the artist legally owns the character designs and can prevent you from using them, sell them to others, or demand ongoing royalties. Essential term 2: Deliverable specification with format requirements (file format, resolution, animation frame count, color mode). Without it, you may receive files in unusable formats or at wrong specifications, requiring rework negotiations. Essential term 3: Milestone-based payment schedule (e.g., 25% upfront, 25% at halfway review, 50% on final delivery). Without it, paying 100% upfront risks the artist disappearing, and paying 100% on delivery risks the artist doing subpar work. Essential term 4: Revision policy (number of included revisions, cost of additional revisions).
