Without it, endless revision requests exhaust the artist, or the artist delivers final work that does not match your vision with no recourse. Essential term 5: Timeline with specific delivery dates and consequences for delays. Without it, the project can stall indefinitely with no recourse.

rubric.key: Work-for-hire/IP transfer identified as most critical with consequences
Deliverable specifications and format requirements with consequences
Payment structure with milestone-based reasoning
Revision policy or timeline terms with consequences

questions.question: Design a zero-budget marketing plan for a solo indie developer's puzzle game launching in 6 months. The developer currently has 200 Twitter followers and no Discord server. Provide a month-by-month timeline with specific weekly actions and realistic growth targets.
questions.answer: Month 1-2: Foundation. Launch Steam Coming Soon page. Create Discord server. Start weekly Twitter posts (3x/week): puzzle solution GIFs, development progress, #ScreenshotSaturday. Begin posting in r/puzzlegames and r/indiegaming (comment genuinely, do not self-promote yet). Target: 500 Twitter followers, 50 Discord members, Steam page live. Month 3-4: Content ramp-up. Start a YouTube/TikTok devlog (bi-weekly, 3-5 minute videos showing development). Share puzzle design insights (value-first content). Submit to 3-4 game festivals. Apply for Steam Next Fest if eligible. Begin building email list through game website. Target: 1,000 Twitter followers, 150 Discord members, 1,000 wishlists, first devlog videos posted. Month 5: Pre-launch push. Increase posting to 5x/week. Demo available on itch.io for feedback. Reach out to 20 puzzle game YouTubers and streamers with personalized emails. Activate email list for launch countdown. Target: 1,500 Twitter followers, 300 Discord members, 3,000 wishlists. Month 6: Launch. Coordinate all channels for launch announcement. Discord community activation. Press and influencer embargo lifts. First-week social media blitz. Target: 5,000+ wishlists at launch, 50+ first-week reviews.

rubric.key: Month-by-month timeline with specific weekly actions
Growth targets are realistic for zero-budget organic growth
Multiple channels used strategically (not just one platform)
Actions escalate appropriately toward launch

questions.question: A developer launched their game in Early Access 3 months ago. It has 200 reviews (72% positive), 800 daily active players, and an active Discord with 2,000 members. The most common negative review complaint is 'not enough content.' Revenue is covering monthly expenses but the developer planned for 18 more months of EA. Evaluate this situation and recommend the next three months of actions.
questions.answer: Evaluation: 72% positive is below the critical 80% threshold where Steam recommendations become significantly less effective. The 'not enough content' feedback is normal for EA but must be addressed before it becomes the dominant narrative. 800 DAU and 2,000 Discord members indicate genuine interest. Revenue covering expenses means the project is sustainable but not thriving. Next three months: Month 1: Ship a significant free content update (new levels/mechanics) to earn a Steam visibility round and demonstrate development momentum. Post a detailed roadmap showing the next 6 months of planned content. Target: review score improvement to 75%+. Month 2: Address the top 3 non-content complaints from negative reviews (QoL improvements, bug fixes, UI polish). Positive reviews from QoL improvements accumulate faster than from new content. Continue regular bi-weekly updates. Target: review score to 78%+. Month 3: Second content update with a new game mode or significant feature. Coordinate with community for feedback on the update before release. Begin preparing for a mid-EA marketing push: updated trailer, refreshed store page screenshots. Target: review score to 80%+, 1,200 DAU.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies 72% as below the critical 80% threshold
Prioritizes content update and roadmap communication
Addresses review improvement through both content and QoL
Realistic targets that improve the situation without promising miracles

questions.question: Compare Kickstarter and Patreon as funding sources for a solo developer's 2-year RPG project. Identify which is more appropriate for this specific situation and explain three reasons why.
questions.answer: For a solo developer's 2-year RPG, Patreon is more appropriate. Reason 1: Sustained funding matches the timeline. A 2-year project needs ongoing income, not a lump sum. Patreon provides monthly income that sustains development over the full timeline. A Kickstarter provides a one-time sum that must last 24 months, creating financial anxiety if development runs long (which it will). Reason 2: Reduced fulfillment risk. Kickstarter creates a legal obligation to deliver specific rewards on a timeline. For a solo developer on a 2-year project, delays are almost certain, and backer frustration compounds. Patreon creates an ongoing relationship where patrons support the process and receive regular value (builds, devlogs, design input). Reason 3: Community building during development. Patreon patrons become the game's earliest community, providing feedback and testing throughout development. Kickstarter backers provide funding upfront but often disengage until delivery, providing less development value. Counter-argument for Kickstarter: if the developer needs a large upfront sum for assets, contractors, or equipment, Kickstarter is better. A hybrid approach (Kickstarter for launch funding, Patreon for ongoing support) is also viable.

rubric.key: Correct selection for the specific scenario with clear reasoning
Sustained funding advantage explained with timeline matching
Fulfillment risk and community building advantages articulated
Acknowledges counter-argument for Kickstarter (intellectual honesty)

questions.question: Compare self-publishing on Steam vs. signing with an indie publisher (like Devolver Digital or Raw Fury) for a first-time indie developer with a puzzle game that has 5,000 wishlists. Analyze the financial and strategic trade-offs for this specific situation.
questions.answer: Self-publishing: Revenue: 70% of Steam gross (after 30% platform fee). With 5,000 wishlists converting at 15-20%, expect 750-1,000 first-week sales. At $15 per copy: $11,250-15,000 gross, $7,875-10,500 net. Full creative control, full ownership. Challenges: no marketing support, no platform relationships, no QA support, and the developer must handle all business operations. Publisher deal (estimated terms): 70/30 or 60/40 split after recoupment of advance ($50K-200K for a mid-tier indie publisher). Publisher provides marketing, PR, QA, localization, and platform porting. The advance funds development; the trade-off is a lower per-unit revenue share. For this specific situation: self-publishing is risky because 5,000 wishlists is on the low end for a strong launch. A publisher could amplify marketing to reach 15,000-20,000 wishlists before launch. The publisher's marketing expertise is the primary value proposition. Recommendation: if the developer has marketing skills and can sustain themselves financially, self-publish. If the developer lacks marketing expertise and needs financial runway, a publisher deal provides both funding and audience amplification. For a first-time developer, a publisher relationship also provides invaluable mentorship.

rubric.key: Financial analysis with specific numbers for both paths
Correctly identifies marketing support as the primary publisher value
Considers developer's specific situation (first-time, 5K wishlists)
Recommendation is justified with clear reasoning for the specific case

questions.question: You participated in a 48-hour game jam and produced a simple physics puzzle game that received enthusiastic feedback. 50 players rated it and 8 left comments asking for a full version. Evaluate whether this concept warrants full development. What additional validation would you seek before committing?
questions.answer: Initial assessment: 50 ratings and 8 full-version requests is a positive signal but an extremely small sample. Enthusiasm from jam players (who are predominantly other developers) may not transfer to the commercial market. Jam games also benefit from low expectations and novelty. The mechanic may or may not sustain a full game. Do not commit to full development based on this alone. Additional validation needed: 1) Competitive analysis: search Steam for similar physics puzzle games. Analyze their review counts, estimated revenue, and market saturation. If the niche is underserved, the signal is stronger. 2) Extended playtest: build a 30-minute prototype with 3-5 puzzle levels and test with 20-30 non-developer players. Measure completion rate, time per puzzle, and ask 'would you pay $10 for a full version of this?' 3) Social media test: post a GIF of the core mechanic on Twitter/X and TikTok. If it generates significantly above-average engagement (saves, shares), the concept has viral visual appeal. 4) Steam Coming Soon page: create the page with the jam footage and description. If it accumulates 500+ wishlists in the first month with minimal promotion, the concept has organic demand. If 3+ of these 4 validations are positive, commit to development with a tight 6-month scope.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies sample size as insufficient for commitment
Multiple validation methods proposed (not just one)
Validation methods include market research and non-developer testing
Defines a clear decision threshold before committing

questions.question: Write a condensed post-mortem for a hypothetical indie game that launched with 80% positive reviews but only 500 first-month sales (projected: 3,000). Identify the most likely root cause and two things that went right.
questions.answer: Root cause analysis: 80% positive reviews indicate the game is good. 500 sales with positive reception is a classic awareness problem, not a quality problem. Most likely root cause: insufficient marketing. The game was probably not visible enough before launch: too few wishlists (likely under 3,000), insufficient influencer/press coverage, no Steam Next Fest participation, and marketing started too late. The game itself converts well (people who find it, like it) but not enough people found it. What went right 1: Game quality. Achieving 80% positive reviews as an indie game means the core design, technical stability, and player experience met expectations. This is the hardest part to get right and the most important foundation. What went right 2: Scope management (implied). The game shipped. It was complete enough to receive positive reviews. The developer successfully managed scope to deliver a finished product, which the majority of indie projects fail to do. Lesson: next project should begin marketing 6-12 months before launch with a Steam Coming Soon page, social media presence, and influencer outreach plan built into the development timeline from day one.

rubric.key: Correctly diagnoses awareness problem, not quality problem
Two genuinely positive 'went right' items with justification
Lesson learned is specific and actionable for the next project
Analysis demonstrates understanding of the reviews-to-sales disconnect

questions.question: A solo indie developer is 8 months into a 12-month project. They are working 60+ hours per week, have stopped exercising, cancelled social plans regularly, and feel constant anxiety about the project. They say they 'just need to push through the last 4 months.' Using the sustainability principles from this course, evaluate this situation and recommend specific changes.
questions.answer: Evaluation: This developer is exhibiting classic burnout symptoms: overwork, social withdrawal, physical health neglect, and anxiety. The belief that 'pushing through' will solve the problem is itself a symptom. At 60+ hours per week, productivity per hour has already declined significantly. The quality of work produced in month 8-12 will be lower than months 1-4, and the developer is at risk of a complete crash that could extend the timeline far beyond 4 months. Recommendations: 1) Immediately reduce to 40-hour weeks. The project will not take longer because the 20+ 'extra' hours are producing little useful output. Enforce a hard stop time daily. 2) Restore one full rest day per week with no game development. Schedule social activity on this day to rebuild the support system. 3) Resume physical exercise (even 20 minutes of walking daily) as a non-negotiable health investment. 4) Reassess scope: at 8 months into a 12-month project with burnout symptoms, the remaining 4 months of work should be reviewed and cut by 30%. Ship what is ready, not what was planned. 5) Schedule a financial checkpoint: if the developer is spending savings, recalculate runway at 16 months (the realistic timeline with sustainable hours) and determine if adjustment is needed.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies burnout symptoms and rejects 'push through' approach
Immediate hour reduction recommendation with productivity reasoning
Rest, social, and physical health recommendations are specific
Scope reassessment and financial checkpoint included

questions.question: You are forming a 2-person indie studio with a friend. You will be the programmer and they will be the artist. Describe the five most important terms to include in your partnership agreement and explain the consequences of omitting each.
questions.answer: Term 1: IP ownership structure. Both partners jointly own the IP, or the studio entity owns it. Without this: if the partnership dissolves, who owns the game? Either partner could claim exclusive ownership, requiring litigation to resolve. The game could be unpublishable during the dispute. Term 2: Revenue split and profit distribution. Define the percentage split (50/50, or weighted by contribution) and when profits are distributed (quarterly, annually). Without this: assumptions about 'fair' splits diverge over time, especially if one partner feels they are contributing more. This is the number one cause of partnership dissolution. Term 3: Decision-making authority and dispute resolution. Define who has final authority on specific decisions (creative, technical, business) and what happens when partners disagree. Without this: deadlocks on important decisions can stall development indefinitely. The partnership becomes a trap where neither person can move forward. Term 4: Exit terms. Define what happens if one partner wants to leave: buyout calculation, IP rights of the departing partner, and transition timeline. Without this: a partner who leaves mid-project could take their contributions (art assets) and the remaining partner has no legal recourse. Term 5: Contribution requirements and accountability. Define the expected weekly hours, milestone commitments, and what constitutes underperformance. Without this: one partner could reduce their contribution while expecting the same revenue share, breeding resentment.

rubric.key: IP ownership identified as critical with dissolution scenario
Revenue split with specific distribution mechanism
Exit terms covering departure and IP retention
Decision authority and contribution requirements addressed

questions.question: An indie developer has $15,000 in savings and needs to decide between: (A) developing full-time for 6 months with no income, or (B) developing part-time while working a 20-hour/week contract job earning $3,000/month for 12 months. Analyze both options assuming $2,500/month expenses and recommend the better choice for a first-time developer.
questions.answer: Option A: Full-time for 6 months. Monthly burn: $2,500. Runway: $15,000 / $2,500 = 6 months exactly. Zero buffer. If development takes 7 months (likely with first-time estimates), the developer runs out of money. Development hours: 40 hrs/week * 26 weeks = 1,040 hours. Risk: very high. No financial safety net. Option B: Part-time with contract work for 12 months. Monthly income: $3,000. Monthly expenses: $2,500. Monthly surplus: $500. After 12 months: $15,000 + ($500 * 12) = $21,000 savings. Development hours: 20 hrs/week * 52 weeks = 1,040 hours. Risk: low. Financial stability maintained. Comparison: both options produce the same number of development hours (1,040). But Option B provides financial stability, doubles the calendar time (more time for marketing to build wishlists), and actually increases savings. Option A provides concentrated focus but creates a financial crisis if anything goes wrong. Recommendation: Option B for a first-time developer. The financial stress of Option A impairs creativity and decision-making. The 12-month timeline also allows for more marketing runway. Option A is only viable for developers with significant prior experience and accurate time estimation.

rubric.key: Correct financial calculations for both options showing equal development hours
Risk assessment favoring Option B for first-time developers
Additional benefits of longer timeline identified (marketing runway)
Acknowledges that Option A could work for experienced developers

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory. More than 2 unexcused absences result in a half-letter grade reduction. Playtesting sessions and guest speaker events cannot be made up. Active participation in peer feedback is required.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. Prototype submissions must include a playable build; documentation alone is insufficient. Extensions may be granted for documented personal circumstances with advance notice.
course_policies.collaboration: All projects are individual unless explicitly stated otherwise. Peer playtesting and feedback is encouraged and expected. Hiring freelancers for art or audio is permitted with disclosure and proper contracts. Code sharing from game jams is permitted with attribution.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All game concepts, design documents, and business plans must be original work. Purchased assets must be licensed and disclosed. AI-generated content must be disclosed. Presenting others' game concepts or business analysis as your own constitutes plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: A game engine (Unity, Godot, or Unreal recommended), image editing software, audio editing capability (Audacity is free), social media accounts, and access to SteamDB/SteamSpy for market research.
